data.source: Laboratory experiments with undergraduate student participants providing survey ratings and evaluations
data.sample_size: Varies by experiment: 54 – 206 per study (e.g., Study 1a n = 132; 1b n = 206; 1c n = 139; 2a n = 117; 2b n = 72; 3 n = 54; 4 n = 102)
data.notes: Participants were primarily undergraduate students at a large public university. Data collected through questionnaires within longer laboratory sessions.

method.description: A series of seven controlled lab experiments examined how branding (brand-level vs. category-level presentation) alters the utilitarian versus symbolic functions associated with product attitudes and how this in turn affects the persuasiveness of function-matching versus mismatching appeals.
steps: Select products previously characterized as supporting predominantly utilitarian or symbolic category-level attitudes (e.g., paper towels, college T-shirts).
Manipulate whether the product is evaluated at the category level (unbranded) or at the brand level (real, fictitious, or unnamed brands).
Measure attitude functions using 7-point benefit and symbolism rating scales for each product.
Compute difference scores (benefit minus symbolism) to index relative utilitarian versus symbolic orientation.
In later studies, present persuasive appeals that either match or mismatch the product’s category-level attitude function (utilitarian vs. symbolic) and record purchase likelihood or ad favorability.

Analyze data using mixed-design ANOVAs, regressions, and simple-effects tests to assess (a) the impact of branding on attitude function and (b) the interaction between branding level and appeal type on persuasion outcomes.
method.models: Mixed-factor ANOVA; multiple regression with interaction terms
method.outcome_variable: 1. Attitude-function ratings (benefit vs. symbolism) 2. Purchase likelihood / advertisement favorability
method.independent_variables: Branding level (category vs. brand), product type (utilitarian vs. symbolic), appeal type (matching vs. mismatching), individual attitude-function difference score (Study 3)
method.control_variables: Specific product/brand, order of presentation, participant counterbalancing

results.summary: Across studies, branding systematically shifted attitude functions: utilitarian product categories became relatively more symbolic when branded, whereas symbolic product categories became relatively more utilitarian. Consequently, persuasive appeals that matched the category-level function outperformed mismatching appeals at the category level but lost — and sometimes reversed — this advantage at the brand level.

numerical_results.effect_size: Difference between category and brand levels

numerical_results.outcome_name: Interaction of product type × level on difference scores (Study 1a)
Level × appeal interaction on purchase likelihood (Study 2a)

metadata.original_paper_title: Branding Alters Attitude Functions and Reduces the Advantage of Function-Matching Persuasive Appeals
